era_context: The Roman Republic is in its final year. Julius Caesar has just been assassinated (March 44 BCE), triggering a succession crisis and civil war. The Senate is divided between Caesar's supporters and his assassins. Mark Antony, Caesar's ally, is consolidating power. Octavian (Caesar's adopted heir) is still a young man but is beginning to assert his claim to Caesar's legacy. The Parthian Empire is stable under Orodes II. This is the pivotal moment that marks the transition from Republic to Principate.

events.title: Assassination of Julius Caesar
events.description: Julius Caesar was assassinated on the Ides of March (March 15, 44 BCE) by a conspiracy of senators led by Brutus and Cassius. Caesar had been granted unprecedented power and was seen by the conspirators as a threat to the Republic. The assassination was intended to restore Republican government, but it instead triggered a series of civil wars that would ultimately destroy the Republic and lead to the Principate. Caesar's death was one of the most consequential political murders in history, with ramifications that would reshape the entire Mediterranean world.

sources.name: Plutarch, Life of Caesar; Life of Brutus

events.certainty_note: The assassination of Caesar is one of the most well-documented events of ancient history. The date (March 15, 44 BCE) is secure from multiple sources. The fact of the assassination is unambiguous.
cross_references: -42-001 (Battle of Philippi, fought to avenge Caesar)

events.title: Mark Antony seizes Caesar's papers and fortune
events.description: In the immediate aftermath of Caesar's assassination, Mark Antony, Caesar's ally and consul, seized Caesar's papers, documents, and fortune. This gave Antony enormous power and resources, as Caesar's will and documents could be used to legitimize various claims and actions. Antony's seizure of Caesar's estate was a crucial step in his consolidation of power and his emergence as a major player in the civil wars that followed. The seizure was controversial, as it was unclear whether Antony was acting in accordance with Caesar's wishes or for his own benefit.
events.certainty_note: Antony's seizure of Caesar's papers and fortune is well-attested in multiple sources. The date is shortly after the assassination (March 44 BCE).

events.title: Octavian learns of his adoption by Caesar; claims his legacy
events.description: Octavian (then known as Gaius Octavius), a young man of about 18 years old, learned that Julius Caesar had adopted him in his will and named him his principal heir. This adoption gave Octavian a claim to Caesar's name, fortune, and political legacy. Octavian immediately began to assert his claim, taking the name Gaius Julius Caesar Octavianus (Octavian). His claim to Caesar's legacy would be the foundation of his rise to power. Octavian's adoption was a turning point in his life and in the history of Rome.
events.certainty_note: Octavian's adoption by Caesar is well-attested in multiple sources. The date is shortly after the assassination (March 44 BCE), though Octavian may not have learned of it immediately.

events.title: Senate grants amnesty to Caesar's assassins; political crisis deepens
events.description: In an attempt to prevent civil war, the Senate granted amnesty to Caesar's assassins (Brutus, Cassius, and others). However, this amnesty was controversial and did not prevent the outbreak of civil war. The amnesty was seen by Caesar's supporters as a betrayal, while the assassins saw it as insufficient protection. The Senate's attempt at reconciliation failed, and the stage was set for the civil wars that would dominate the next decade.
events.certainty_note: The Senate's grant of amnesty is well-attested in multiple sources. The date is shortly after the assassination (March 44 BCE).
cross_references: -42-001 (Battle of Philippi, where the amnesty was violated)

events.title: Cicero delivers first Philippic against Mark Antony
events.description: Marcus Tullius Cicero, the greatest orator of the Roman Republic, delivered the first of his Philippics—a series of speeches attacking Mark Antony. Cicero opposed Antony's consolidation of power and his apparent sympathy for Caesar's assassins. The Philippics were named after Demosthenes' speeches against Philip of Macedon and became some of the most famous speeches in history. Cicero's attacks on Antony would eventually lead to his proscription and execution in 43 BCE.
events.certainty_note: Cicero's Philippics are preserved as primary texts. The first Philippic was delivered in September 44 BCE, making it a contemporary source.
cross_references: -44-001 (Assassination of Caesar, which prompted Cicero's opposition to Antony)
-43-003 (Cicero's execution, result of his opposition to Antony)

events.title: Cleopatra VII gives birth to Caesarion
events.description: Cleopatra VII, Queen of Egypt, gave birth to a son, Ptolemy Caesar (Caesarion), whom she claimed was the biological son of Julius Caesar. The birth of Caesarion was politically significant, as it gave Cleopatra a claim to Caesar's legacy and a potential heir who could unite Egypt and Rome. However, Caesarion's legitimacy was disputed, and he would eventually be executed by Octavian. The birth of Caesarion was a major event in Egyptian politics and had ramifications for the future of the Mediterranean world.

sources.name: Josephus, Antiquities of the Jews, Book XV

events.certainty_note: The birth of Caesarion is attested in multiple sources, but the exact date is uncertain. Most sources place it in 47 BCE, but some place it in 44 BCE. The paternity is disputed; Cleopatra claimed Caesar was the father, but this is not confirmed by contemporary sources.

disconfirming_evidence: The date of Caesarion's birth is disputed; most modern scholars place it in 47 BCE rather than 44 BCE, making it contemporary with Caesar's visit to Egypt in 48–47 BCE. The paternity of Caesarion is highly disputed; while Cleopatra claimed Caesar was the father, there is no contemporary evidence to confirm this. Some modern scholars argue that Caesarion was the son of another man and that Cleopatra falsely claimed Caesar as the father for political reasons. The Senate's grant of amnesty to Caesar's assassins is sometimes portrayed as a failure of the Senate to maintain order, but some modern scholars argue it was a reasonable attempt at reconciliation given the circumstances. Cicero's Philippics are preserved as primary texts, but they are polemical speeches designed to attack Antony, not objective historical accounts.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 44 BCE is rich, drawing on a combination of primary texts (Cicero's Philippics) and later compilations by Appian, Plutarch, Dio Cassius, and Suetonius. The assassination of Caesar is one of the most thoroughly documented events of ancient history, but the sources reflect the perspectives of the victors and the defeated. The Philippics are primary texts but are polemical in nature. The sources are heavily focused on Rome and the Mediterranean; events in the Parthian Empire, India, and China are not covered due to source limitations in the Western tradition. The narrative of the assassination is sometimes sensationalized in later sources, particularly Plutarch and Appian. The date of Caesarion's birth is uncertain and is a matter of scholarly debate.

graph_edges.note: The assassination of Caesar led to the formation of the Second Triumvirate.
The assassination of Caesar led to the Battle of Philippi, fought to avenge his death.
Antony's seizure of Caesar's fortune gave him the resources to form the triumvirate.
Octavian's claim to Caesar's legacy led to his joining the triumvirate.
Cicero's opposition to Antony in the Philippics led to his proscription and execution.
